title: The Warrior Life of Ancient Egyptian Princesses
author: Jake Currie
text: Subscribe to skip adsAdvertisementAnthropologyThe Warrior Life of Ancient Egyptian PrincessesThese weren’t your traditional Disney princessesJCBy Jake Currie1:00 PM CDT on July 17, 2026Share on FacebookShare on X (formerly Twitter)Share on RedditShare on EmailShare on BlueskyNo matter how absent-minded you are, you’ve probably never misplaced an entire mummy. Some unfortunate soul once lost six of them, and at least three of those were princesses. The mummies, discovered in 1890 in the pyramids in Dahshur, were later re-discovered during a museum curation project in 2020. Now they’re the royal subjects of a new study published in Frontiers in Environmental Archaeology. And they’re no ordinary princesses. Subscribe to skip adsFeatured VideoPowered By 10 Flip-Flops of the Future! Algae-Based Shoes for the WinShareNextStayWARRIOR PRINCESS: The dagger found buried with Princess Ita. Photo courtesy of the Egyptian Museum.Among the mummified remains were the daughters of pharaoh Amenemhat II: Princess Ita, Princess Khenmet, and Princess Itaweret (a fourth is most likely Princess Sathathormeryt, but the researchers can’t say for sure). Like many mummies of both genders, they were buried alongside a cache of weapons: daggers, bows, and arrows. Whether these armaments were used in real life or merely ceremonial has been the subject of some debate. Already have an account?Log inJoin to continue readingYou've run out of free articles.
